portainer.public_desc: Browser-facing URL for "Open in Portainer" deep links. May differ from the API URL when Portainer sits behind a reverse proxy.
portainer.swarm_autoheal_hint: When the swarm_agent_health schedule kind detects an unhealthy Portainer agent, what should happen? Notify-only fires the existing notification event. Auto-restart additionally spawns a do_restart_swarm_agent op subject to the cooldown tunable.
portainer.swarm_autoheal_bootstrap_hint: On first boot, when Portainer is configured AND no equivalent row exists, OmniGrid auto-creates a 5-minute swarm_agent_health schedule so the autoheal action fires without manual setup. Disable this BEFORE the first boot if you prefer to manage the schedule yourself. Once a schedule exists (auto or manual), this toggle has no further effect — deleting the auto-created row leaves it deleted.
portainer.tunables_intro: Wall-clock caps for the three categories of Portainer write-op. Short covers fast endpoints (container restart, inspect). Medium covers service-level ops (restart service, prune node, restart Swarm agent). Long covers image-pull-heavy ops (stack update, container update, retag-to-latest). Lower to fail-fast on a flaky agent; raise on slow networks / large stacks. All edits commit through the Portainer Save button (no fresh Test required — tunable edits don't affect connectivity).

ignores.title: Ignore list
ignores.intro_prefix: Items matching these patterns show as

oidc.title: Authentik OIDC (SSO)
oidc.tunables_intro: Outbound HTTP wall-clock for every OIDC call — discovery-doc GET, JWKS fetch, token exchange at callback, and the admin Test-connection probe. Lower on fast-fail watchdog deploys; raise when the IdP sits behind a slow corporate proxy or VPN. All edits commit through the OIDC Save button.
oidc.description: OpenID Connect provider. Works with any compliant IdP; the label says Authentik because that's our reference setup.
oidc.enable_toggle: Enable OIDC login (shows the "Sign in with Authentik" button on /login)
oidc.issuer_hint: Paste the "OpenID Configuration Issuer" URL from your Authentik provider's Metadata tab. We append /.well-known/openid-configuration to it automatically.
oidc.client_secret_placeholder: Paste new secret or leave blank
oidc.client_secret_clear_text: Clear the saved Authentik client secret? OIDC sign-in will fail until you save a new one.
oidc.redirect_uri_note: (paste this into Authentik's Redirect URIs allowlist)
oidc.redirect_uri_hint: Computed from the current request's origin. Authentik must have this exact value in its "Redirect URIs" allowlist, one per line.
oidc.scopes_hint: Space-separated. groups is required for role mapping.
oidc.admin_group_hint: Group membership is re-checked on every OIDC login. Users outside this group land as readonly.
oidc.verify_tls_title: Verify TLS on discovery / token / JWKS calls
oidc.verify_tls_hint: Leave on when the issuer has a publicly-trusted cert. Turn off for homelab issuers behind an internal CA whose root isn't in the container's trust store (e.g. *.example.com with a self-signed PKI). Mirrors the behaviour of Portainer's Verify TLS toggle.
oidc.group_case_sensitive_hint: Leave on (default) for byte-exact matching of the admin group claim. Turn off when the IdP returns mixed-case group names that don't match the value you typed above (e.g. you typed OmniGrid-Admins but Authentik returns omnigrid-admins). Off compares both sides lowered.
oidc.save_requires_test: Run a successful Test connection first — Save is gated until the issuer URL discovery + JWKS round-trip works.

shortcuts.title: Keyboard shortcuts
shortcuts.hint: Press <kbd class="kbd">?</kbd> anywhere to open the full reference.

admin.intro: User accounts, active sessions, and API tokens. Destructive actions prompt for confirmation.

nodes.prune_prompt_html:
Run docker system prune -af --volumes on {host}?

This deletes all stopped containers, unused networks, dangling images, and unreferenced volumes. Active services are not affected.

general.title: General
general.subtitle: UI-side service settings that don't fit auth, hosts, scheduling, or notifications. Today: Open-Meteo (topbar weather widget). Future home for any other ambient/UI integrations.

ai.heading: AI Integration
ai.intro: Connect OmniGrid to a large-language-model provider for future AI-assisted features. Stage 1: foundation only — provider configuration plus a dashboard for usage / cost / accuracy. Wiring the actual AI calls comes in a later stage.
ai.chip_ok_title: Provider is enabled and an API key is configured.
ai.chip_no_key_title: Provider is enabled but no API key is saved — calls will fail with HTTP 401 until a key is added.
ai.chip_disabled_title: Provider is disabled. Enable it in its card to start using it.
ai.current_model_title: The model id stored on the provider's settings card. Historical sub-rows below show every model that recorded usage in the selected period.
ai.master_hint_disabled: (no provider will be called until enabled)
ai.active_provider_hint: The provider that any future AI-backed feature will route through. You can keep credentials on file for every provider — only the active one is used.
ai.active_provider_disabled_warning: The active provider is currently disabled — enable it in its card below before any AI call can succeed.
ai.max_tokens_hint: Maximum tokens the model is allowed to emit per response. Applied to every provider. Default 1024 (clamped 64–32000). Lower to cap costs on a paid tier; raise when responses keep coming back truncated.
ai.tunables_intro: Every numeric / boolean knob below routes through the TUNABLES system (DB > env > default + bounds-clamp). Covers output-token cap, transient-overload retry, provider fallback depth, sidebar layout, conversation-persist cadence, log-context window, and outbound HTTP timeouts. All edits flip the Save button's amber ring; one Save commits everything in a single round-trip alongside the master toggle / active provider / per-provider credentials.

retry.heading: Auto-retry on transient overload
retry.help: When an AI call hits HTTP 429 / 502 / 503 / 504 (rate-limit or upstream-overloaded — e.g. Gemini's 'high demand' message) AND the first attempt was fast, retry once after a short backoff before propagating the failure to the modal. Auth failures, model-not-found, payload-too-large, and other 4xx / 5xx errors do NOT retry — they're operator-fixable.

fallback.heading: Provider fallback chain
fallback.help: When the active provider returns a transient overload (HTTP 429 / 502 / 503 / 504) AND the auto-retry single-retry has already exhausted, transparently try the next provider in the chain before surfacing the failure. Eligibility: every provider in the chain must be master-enabled AND have an API key configured. The active primary cannot be in the chain. Limit hops to at most 2 — beyond that a multi-provider outage cascades into 4× latency for no real recovery upside. Disabled by default — opt-in once you've reviewed the cost implications (a fallback from a cheap provider to an expensive one can multiply per-call cost by 50× or more silently).
fallback.enable_label: Enable provider fallback when the active one is overloaded
fallback.no_eligible_warning: No alternate provider is currently eligible (need ≥ 1 provider that is master-enabled AND has an API key set AND isn't the active primary). Configure one in the cards below for fallback to actually fire.
fallback.max_depth_hint: 1 = try the first backup only; 2 = try first then second backup before giving up.
fallback.chain_hint: First in the list = first to try when the active provider is overloaded. Use the up/down arrows to reorder.
fallback.ineligible_hint: (disabled or missing API key — can't be a backup until you fix that on its card below)

ai.providers_intro: Each provider has its own enable toggle, model id, optional self-hosted base URL, and API key. API keys are write-only — saved keys round-trip as 'set' / 'not set' only and never leave the server.
ai.model_hint_claude: Anthropic model id (e.g. claude-opus-4-7, claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-haiku-4-5).
ai.model_hint_gemini: Google Gemini model id (e.g. gemini-2.5-pro, gemini-2.5-flash).
ai.base_url_hint: Override the provider's default API host — leave blank for the canonical endpoint. Useful for self-hosted proxies or compatibility shims.
ai.base_url_http_warning: Cleartext: API key will be sent unencrypted over HTTP. Use https:// unless this is a same-host service.
ai.no_data: No AI calls recorded yet — the dashboard fills in once Stage 2+ wires the call wrapper.

modal.cost_desc: Hourly cost in USD, summed across every provider.
modal.tokens_desc: Hourly token consumption across every provider.
modal.response_time_desc: Mean response time per hour bucket — emerges as data accumulates.
modal.accuracy_desc: Hourly mean accuracy score, when validation has run.
modal.empty_jobs: No AI calls recorded in this window.
modal.empty_trend: No trend data yet — accumulates once calls are made.

notifications.tunables_intro: Knobs scoped to the in-app notifications surface — DB retention window (how long to keep entries), per-page count for the popup pagination, and SPA poll cadence. All edits commit through the notifications Save button.
notifications.tabs_intro: Pick the channel you want to configure. In-app keeps notifications inside OmniGrid; Apprise relays them to any of 100+ external services via webhook; Telegram delivers them to a Telegram chat / supergroup / topic. Per-event toggles below apply to every enabled channel uniformly.
notifications.tab_app_intro: OmniGrid's built-in notifications page (the bell icon in the topbar). Always on — every event lands here regardless of whether external channels are configured. The retention / page-size / poll-cadence tunables below control how long entries survive in the DB and how the popup paginates.
notifications.tab_telegram_intro: Send notifications to a Telegram chat. Works with direct messages (positive user_id), groups (negative chat_id), supergroups (chat_id prefixed with -100), and forum topics (set the optional Topic ID below). Phase 1 = send-only; inbound commands arrive in a future phase.
notifications.medium_apprise_fanout_label: Fan notifications out to Apprise on every enabled event
notifications.telegram_bot_token_hint: Get this from @BotFather on Telegram (/newbot, then /token if you misplace it). The bot needs at minimum "send messages" permission in the destination chat / supergroup.
notifications.telegram_chat_id_hint: Direct messages: positive user_id (your own Telegram numeric id). Groups / supergroups / channels: negative integer, typically prefixed with -100. Get it by inviting your bot to the destination, sending /id, or via @userinfobot for personal DMs.
notifications.telegram_thread_id_hint: Only set this when the destination is a supergroup with topics (forum mode) enabled AND you want notifications routed to a specific topic. Leave blank to post to the supergroup's main feed.
notifications.medium_help: Each enabled event fans out to every channel below. Turn one off to silence it without disabling events globally — for example keep in-app on while quieting Apprise during a maintenance window.

events.title: Per-event notifications
events.subtitle: Enable / disable specific notification events. Test notifications always send regardless of these toggles.
events.test_always_sends: Test notifications always send regardless of these toggles.
events.cat_count_title: Events globally enabled in this category

notify_templates.modal_subtitle: Customise the title and body of this notification. Use the placeholder chips to insert dynamic values.
notify_templates.edit_button_title: Click to edit this notification's template

placeholder_help.name: Target's display name (stack / container / service / host).
placeholder_help.actor: User who triggered the operation, or 'system' for sampler-fired events.
placeholder_help.host: Resolved hostname for ops that target one host.
placeholder_help.time: ISO 8601 timestamp (UTC) of when the notification fired.
placeholder_help.error: Error message (failure events only). Truncated to 500 characters.
placeholder_help.message: Body content for warning / informational events. Always populated regardless of severity (use this in templates instead of {error} for non-failure events).

config.title: Process tunables
config.subtitle: Override the {count} runtime knobs that would otherwise require an env-var change + restart. Each value resolves via DB setting → env var → code default. Leave a field blank to clear the override and fall back to the env / default shown in the placeholder. UI changes take effect on the next consumer read — TTLs immediate, samplers on the next tick.

bounds.default_title: Default value (used when both the DB override and the env var are blank)

errors.must_be_int: {field} must be a whole number (no decimals).

tuning_cache_ttl_seconds.label: Items cache TTL (seconds)
tuning_cache_ttl_seconds.help: How long the /api/items result (services + containers + stacks + remote registry digests) stays valid before the next caller triggers a fresh _gather() pass. Drives the Stacks / Services tables. Lower it if you want "check for new image digests" to feel more live (cost: every call past TTL pays the full registry-digest sweep, ~5-30s on a fleet with many private images). Raise it to lighten outbound load on Docker Hub / private registries when you don't need fresh digest data more than once every N minutes. Default 900s (15 min). The SPA's force-refresh hotkey (Shift+R) and the auto-refresh ticker bypass this cache via ?force=true.

tuning_stats_cache_ttl_seconds.label: Stats cache TTL (seconds)
tuning_stats_cache_ttl_seconds.help: How long the /api/stats result (per-container CPU% / memory / image disk size) stays valid before the next caller triggers a fresh stats poll. This is a SEPARATE cache from items so the SPA's 15s stats-poll doesn't keep refreshing the slow registry-digest sweep. Lower it to make per-row CPU / Memory bars feel more live (cost: more parallel /containers/{id}/stats calls to the Docker daemon via Portainer). Raise it on a large fleet to cut Docker daemon load. Default 30s — matches the SPA's 30s polling interval so most polls are cache hits.

tuning_registry_concurrency.label: Registry digest concurrency
tuning_registry_concurrency.help: Maximum parallel HEAD requests OmniGrid fires at remote container registries (Docker Hub, ghcr, registry.k8s.io, your private registry) per _gather() pass to resolve each image's :tag → @sha256 digest. The semaphore caps the concurrent in-flight requests so a fleet with 100 unique images doesn't open 100 sockets at once. Lower it when a registry rate-limits you (Docker Hub anonymous pulls cap aggressively — symptom: 429 / 401 in [registry] log lines). Raise it on a beefy network with multiple registries so the digest pass finishes faster. Default 8.

tuning_stats_concurrency.label: Container stats concurrency
tuning_stats_concurrency.help: Maximum parallel /containers/{id}/stats?stream=false calls to the Docker daemon (via Portainer) during one stats gather. Each call costs ~50-200ms, so a fleet with 80 running containers can take 5+ seconds even at this concurrency. Lower it on a slow / loaded Swarm manager so the stats sweep doesn't compete with normal Docker scheduling. Raise it on a fast manager with many containers so the sweep finishes inside the SPA's 30s poll cadence. Default 16.

tuning_stats_targeted_timeout_seconds.label: Stats fetch timeout — agent-targeted (seconds)
tuning_stats_targeted_timeout_seconds.help: How long to wait for /containers/{id}/stats?stream=false when the call carries an X-PortainerAgent-Target header (forwarded to a specific Swarm node). Bumped from 4s to 12s after operator-reported worker-node stats coming back empty: Portainer's agent forwarding can take longer than 4s on busy nodes, the call would time out, the untargeted fallback would 404 (manager doesn't have the worker's cid), and the UI rendered as `—` even though the agent would have responded if given more time. Lower it if your Portainer agents are fast and you want to fail-fast on dead workers. Raise it if you see repeated TimeoutError in the [stats] logs. Range 1..60s. Default 12s.

tuning_stats_untargeted_timeout_seconds.label: Stats fetch timeout — untargeted (seconds)
tuning_stats_untargeted_timeout_seconds.help: How long to wait for /containers/{id}/stats?stream=false when the call has NO X-PortainerAgent-Target header (asks the manager's daemon directly). Used as a fallback when the targeted call fails — manager-local containers respond directly here; worker-node cids return 404 (manager doesn't have them, no agent to route via). Lower it to fail-fast on workers; raise it if your manager is slow. Range 1..60s. Default 10s.

tuning_swarm_agent_unhealthy_threshold.label: Swarm agent unhealthy threshold (gather cycles)
tuning_swarm_agent_unhealthy_threshold.help: Number of consecutive gather cycles where a Swarm node had >=1 running task container but ZERO successful /stats calls before the SPA flags the node's Portainer agent as unhealthy via the banner in the Stacks / Hosts views. Default 3 — covers transient hub blips without spamming the banner. Lower for faster operator feedback (1 = banner on first bad gather, can flicker). Raise for noisy fleets where one bad gather isn't worth surfacing. Range 1..20.

tuning_stats_history_days.label: Time-series retention (days)
tuning_stats_history_days.help: How long every time-series table (stats_samples, host_net_samples, host_metrics_samples) keeps rows before the hourly pruner deletes them. Drives the inline sparklines on the Stacks view + the Hosts drawer's CPU / Memory / Disk / Net history charts. Lower it when SQLite is growing too large on a small disk (each sample row is ~80 bytes, but a 60-host fleet sampled every 5min for 30d adds up). Raise it for longer-term graphs. Default 7d. Bumping this RETROACTIVELY surfaces older data immediately (no backfill — rows already exist, the cutoff just moves).

tuning_stats_sample_interval_seconds.label: Sampler interval (seconds)
tuning_stats_sample_interval_seconds.help: Cadence for every lifespan-managed sampler: container stats → stats_samples, node-exporter network counters → host_net_samples, full host metrics → host_metrics_samples. Lower it for finer-grained sparklines + faster response in the Net I/O fallback chart (cost: N× more rows in SQLite for the same retention window — re-tune `tuning_stats_history_days` together if you halve this). Raise it on a Pi / underpowered host to reduce sampler CPU. Default 300s (5 min). Takes effect on the NEXT tick after Save (current sleep finishes first).

tuning_host_baseline_recompute_interval_.label: Host baseline recompute interval (seconds)
tuning_host_baseline_recompute_interval_.help: Cadence at which the lifespan `host_baseline_sampler` walks every curated host and refreshes its rolling 30-day median + IQR baseline for drift detection (▲ / ▼ / ━ chips on each Hosts row). Default 3600s (hourly). Baselines move SLOWLY (30-day window — yesterday's data shifts the median by ~3% in a steady fleet) so finer cadence wastes DB cycles. High-churn fleets (workload changes weekly) may want 1800s; stable fleets (workload monotonic) may dial up to 21600s (6h) to free DB cycles for other samplers. Range 60..86400. Takes effect on the next tick after Save (current sleep finishes first).

tuning_host_baseline_first_tick_delay_se.label: Host baseline first-tick delay (seconds)
tuning_host_baseline_first_tick_delay_se.help: How long the host-baseline sampler waits after lifespan-start before the first tick fires. Gives schema migrations (the additive `host_baselines` CREATE TABLE in init_db) time to land before the sampler reads / writes the table. Default 60s. Raise it on a heavily-loaded boot where migrations take longer than a minute; lower it to start baselining sooner on a fast container. Range 5..600. Takes effect on the next container restart (the value is read once when the sampler starts).

tuning_kick_gather_timeout_seconds.label: Cold-cache gather kick timeout (seconds)
tuning_kick_gather_timeout_seconds.help: Wall-clock cap for the cold-cache `_gather()` call that drill-down endpoints (like the Stats → Samples per-(table, host_id) drill-in) trigger when `_cache["items"]` is empty. Without this cap an unreachable Portainer could hang the endpoint indefinitely. Default 30s — enough headroom for a typical Portainer fan-out + registry digest probe. Operators with large fleets / slow registries hit it during incidents; bump to 60-120s in those environments. Range 5..300. Takes effect on the next drill-down lookup.

tuning_portainer_op_timeout_short_second.label: Portainer op timeout — short (seconds)
tuning_portainer_op_timeout_short_second.help: Wall-clock cap for quick Portainer write-ops — container restart + container remove. Default 120s. Lower it to fail-fast on a flaky agent; raise it on slow networks. Range 10..600. Takes effect on the next op.

tuning_portainer_op_timeout_medium_secon.label: Portainer op timeout — medium (seconds)
tuning_portainer_op_timeout_medium_secon.help: Wall-clock cap for service-level Portainer write-ops — restart service, restart Swarm agent, prune node. Default 300s. Range 30..1800. Takes effect on the next op.

tuning_portainer_op_timeout_long_seconds.label: Portainer op timeout — long (seconds)
tuning_portainer_op_timeout_long_seconds.help: Wall-clock cap for image-pull-heavy Portainer write-ops — stack update, container update, container retag-to-latest. Default 600s. Raise on slow registries / large stacks. Range 60..3600. Takes effect on the next op.

tuning_asset_inventory_token_timeout_sec.label: Asset inventory — token probe timeout (seconds)
tuning_asset_inventory_token_timeout_sec.help: Wall-clock cap for the OAuth2 `client_credentials` token-probe handshake against the asset-inventory API. Default 10s. Operators behind a slow corporate network or with the asset API behind a tunnel can bump this to 30-60s; tight-watchdog deploys can lower to 3-5s. Range 2..120. Takes effect on the next probe.

tuning_asset_inventory_fetch_timeout_sec.label: Asset inventory — asset fetch timeout (seconds)
tuning_asset_inventory_fetch_timeout_sec.help: Wall-clock cap for the paginated `GET /assets` (and lifetime-token equivalent) fetch against the asset-inventory API. Default 15s. Large fleets / slow upstreams may need 60-120s; the cache is manual-refresh only so the timeout only fires when the operator clicks Refresh. Range 2..300. Takes effect on the next fetch.

tuning_ai_http_timeout_seconds.label: AI provider — Test-connection timeout (seconds)
tuning_ai_http_timeout_seconds.help: Wall-clock cap for the lightweight one-token Test-connection probe that fires when the admin clicks Test in Admin → AI Integration. Default 15s. Slow networks or cold-start models may need 30-60s; tight-watchdog deploys can lower to 5-10s. Range 2..120. Takes effect on the next Test click.

tuning_ai_extended_http_timeout_seconds.label: AI provider — chat-completion timeout (seconds)
tuning_ai_extended_http_timeout_seconds.help: Wall-clock cap for the real chat-completion call (Cmd-K palette, AI sidebar, host-drawer chart questions). Default 30s. Long-context prompts or slower reasoning models may need 60-120s; quick-fire workflows can lower to 15-20s. Range 5..300. Takes effect on the next call.

tuning_host_permanent_fail_window_second.label: Host sampling pause window (seconds)
tuning_host_permanent_fail_window_second.help: After this many seconds of CONSECUTIVE probe failures (no successful sample landed in this window), the host_metrics_sampler stops sampling that host entirely — no more probe attempts, no more failure rows in the log. The host shows a paused badge in the Hosts table and an explanatory banner in the drawer. Resume manually via the drawer's "Resume sampling" button. Lower it to react faster to dead hosts (less log noise from a host that crashed); raise it to tolerate longer transient outages without auto-pausing (e.g. a host that reboots every Sunday for an hour). Default 900s (15 min).

tuning_ops_poll_interval_seconds.label: Ops poll cadence (seconds)
tuning_ops_poll_interval_seconds.help: How often the SPA polls /api/ops while it's open to detect when a background operation (stack update, container restart, prune, etc.) finishes. Drives the live progress chip + auto-refresh after success. When SSE is connected (Live mode), the cadence stretches to a 30s safety-net keepalive because op deltas arrive via push instead. Lower this (e.g. 1) for snappier feedback on a click → "Done" cycle (cost: more idle GET /api/ops requests when no ops are running); raise it (e.g. 5–10) to cut idle traffic on a long-open SPA tab. Default 2 seconds. Internally stored as seconds and multiplied × 1000 before being delivered to the SPA's setTimeout via `/api/me`'s `client_config.ops_poll_ms`. Takes effect on the next /api/me round-trip.

tuning_log_retention_days.label: Log retention (days)
tuning_log_retention_days.help: How long persistent log files at /app/data/logs/omnigrid-YYYY-MM-DD.log are kept before the hourly pruner sweep deletes them. The in-memory ring buffer in Admin → Logs (Live tab) is INDEPENDENT — it always shows the last 2000 lines from the current process and is unaffected by this setting. Lower it if disk space is tight; raise it for longer audit trails / forensic windows. Default 7d. Pruner clamps to (1, 365) at consume time so a corrupt DB row can't disable the sweep entirely.

tuning_notification_retention_days.label: Notifications retention (days)
tuning_notification_retention_days.help: How long rows in the in-app notifications store are kept before the prune_notifications schedule kind deletes them. Drives the badge / Notifications page time horizon. Independent from log retention so operators can keep a multi-month notifications trail (audit / quarterly review) without inflating the daily log file disk usage. Lower it on a tiny SQLite footprint; raise it for longer audit trails. Default 90 days. Pruner clamps to the (1, 3650) range at consume time so a corrupt DB row can't disable the sweep entirely. Takes effect on the next prune_notifications run; a manual Run-now from Admin → Schedules applies the new value immediately.

tuning_notification_page_size.label: Notifications page size
tuning_notification_page_size.help: How many notifications the in-app popup loads per fetch. Default 25 — operators on busy fleets where every gather emits new entries reported the previous 50 felt overwhelming. Lower it (toward 5) for a tighter scroll-less popup; raise it (toward 200) when you want to see a longer slice of recent events without paginating. Range 5..200 enforced at write-time + clamped at read-time. Takes effect on the next /api/me round-trip + the next time the popup opens (existing open popup keeps its current value until reload).

tuning_ai_retry_enabled.label: AI auto-retry enabled
tuning_ai_retry_enabled.help: Master toggle for the auto-retry behaviour on transient AI provider overloads (HTTP 429 / 502 / 503 / 504). When enabled, the call retries ONCE after a short backoff IF the first attempt was fast (see the first-attempt-max gate below). When disabled, the first failure propagates straight to the modal — useful when you find the retry-induced 2-second wait more annoying than the 'high demand' modal pop-up itself. Stored as 0 / 1; default 1 (enabled).

tuning_ai_retry_backoff_ms.label: AI retry backoff (ms)
tuning_ai_retry_backoff_ms.help: Sleep duration before the retry attempt. Default 2000 ms = 2 seconds — short enough that the user's typing rhythm isn't broken, long enough that a transient overload usually clears. Range 0..30000. Set to 0 to retry immediately (useful for tests); raise on slow upstreams under sustained load. Only applies when the master toggle above is enabled.

tuning_ai_retry_first_attempt_max_ms.label: Retry only when first attempt was faster than (ms)
tuning_ai_retry_first_attempt_max_ms.help: First-attempt-duration gate. The retry only fires when the FIRST attempt resolved within this many milliseconds — if the first attempt was already slow, the upstream is genuinely struggling and a retry won't help (just doubles the wait). Default 5000 ms = 5 seconds. Range 100..60000. Lower it on a fast home-lab where any slow first attempt should NOT be retried; raise it when the upstream is normally slow but transient overloads are the dominant failure mode.

tuning_ai_max_tokens.label: Max response tokens
tuning_ai_max_tokens.help: Output-token cap per AI request — applied to every provider's chat-completion call. Bumped when responses keep coming back truncated; lowered to cap costs on a paid tier. Note: Gemini 2.5 family has an internal "thinking" mode that consumes output tokens BEFORE producing visible text; on `gemini-2.5-pro` a 1024-token budget often surfaces as "MAX_TOKENS after 0 output tokens" because the thinking phase exhausted the budget. Raise to 4096+ when using Gemini 2.5 Pro, OR switch to `gemini-2.5-flash` (our backend disables Flash's thinking mode automatically). Range 16..16384. Default 1024.

tuning_ai_fallback_max_depth.label: Fallback chain max depth
tuning_ai_fallback_max_depth.help: When the active AI provider returns a transient-overload status (HTTP 429 / 502 / 503 / 504) OR times out, walk this many backup providers from the operator-defined fallback chain before surfacing the failure. Default 1 (try ONE backup); 2 tolerates a multi-provider outage at the cost of doubling user-perceived latency on a true outage. Range 0..3 (0 effectively disables the fallback chain even when the master toggle is on).

tuning_ai_log_context_hours.label: Log context window (hours)
tuning_ai_log_context_hours.help: How many hours of persistent log files the AI palette injects into the per-call context. Default 168 (7 days). Pre-fix the AI saw only the in-memory ring buffer's last 30 lines — minutes of coverage on a busy fleet, leading to replies like 'I don't have access to the full 24-hour history'. Reads from the persistent log files via `recent_lines_window(hours=N)`, filtered to error+warn lines, capped at the line-count limit below. Lower this if your fleet writes heavy logs and you want to bound the prompt token cost; raise to 720 (30 days) if your fleet is quiet and you want longer historical context. Range 1..720.

tuning_ai_log_context_lines.label: Log context max lines
tuning_ai_log_context_lines.help: Hard upper bound on the number of error+warn log lines injected per AI palette call, newest-last so the most recent issues survive trimming when the window contains more lines than this cap. Default 200 — comfortable for a week's worth of signal on a moderately-busy fleet without ballooning the prompt. Lower to 50-100 if costs are a concern; raise to 500-1000 only when you've confirmed the prompt budget can absorb it (logs >200 chars are also truncated per-line). Range 10..2000.

tuning_backup_retention_count.label: Backup retention count
tuning_backup_retention_count.help: Number of recent backup zips to keep on disk under `/app/data/backups/`. The `backup` schedule kind purges older files once this count is exceeded. Default 0 = keep ALL backups (back-compat with pre-existing deploys). Operator typically wants 7-30 to bound disk growth on a daily schedule. Range 0..1000.

tuning_config_backup_retention_count.label: Config-backup retention count
tuning_config_backup_retention_count.help: Number of recent Settings-as-Code JSON snapshots to keep on disk under `/app/data/config_backups/`. The `config_backup` schedule kind purges older files once this count is exceeded. Default 30 = roughly one month at daily cadence. Set 0 = unlimited (back-compat with the backup-zip retention default). Range 0..1000.

tuning_ssh_ws_heartbeat_seconds.label: SSH WebSocket heartbeat (seconds)
tuning_ssh_ws_heartbeat_seconds.help: Server-side ping interval for the SSH terminal WebSocket — keeps the connection alive past idle-timeouts in NPM / openresty. Default 25 seconds (under the typical 30 s `proxy_read_timeout`). Lower if your reverse proxy has a tighter idle timeout; raise on long-lived sessions to cut traffic. Range 5..120. Takes effect on the NEXT iteration without reconnect.

tuning_host_snapshots_cache_ttl_seconds.label: Host snapshots cache TTL (seconds)
tuning_host_snapshots_cache_ttl_seconds.help: Short-TTL read cache for the host_snapshots SQLite table — the source of stale-fallback data when a provider goes offline mid-session. The SPA fans out N parallel /api/hosts/one/{id} calls per refresh, each of which would otherwise issue its own SELECT against host_snapshots; caching collapses N reads into 1 per TTL window. Cache is auto-busted on every snapshot WRITE so write→read consistency is immediate (no stale-data risk). Lower it / set to 0 to disable the cache entirely (every call hits the DB — only useful for debugging cache-related stale-data bugs). Raise it on a large fleet where the parallel SELECT stampede shows up in profiling. Default 5s.

tuning_host_snapshot_stale_field_max_age.label: Host snapshot stale-field grace (hours)
tuning_host_snapshot_stale_field_max_age.help: How long a host_* field can stay restored from the snapshot fallback before it's dropped as an orphan. When a provider goes down, the snapshot fallback restores its last-known value flagged stale; without a cap, fields a host's active providers can NEVER produce (e.g. host_cpu_percent on an APC UPS, host_ping_loss_pct on a non-pinged host) accumulate forever — every gather restores them, every save re-persists them. With this cap, after `now - first_stale_ts > grace_window` the field is dropped from BOTH the merged dict AND the next saved snapshot, so the orphan decays naturally. Lower (e.g. 6h) on a fleet where you'd rather see 'no data' fast than tolerate phantom-stale rows; raise (e.g. 168h / 7d) on a fleet where transient outages routinely last >24h. Default 24h. Range 1..720h (1h..30d). Takes effect on the NEXT gather without restart.

tuning_hosts_parallel_fetch.label: Hosts fan-out concurrency
tuning_hosts_parallel_fetch.help: Cap on the SPA's PARALLEL /api/hosts/one/{id} calls during a Hosts-tab load / refresh. The SPA fetches the curated host list first (fast skeleton), then fans out per-host probes with this concurrency. Lower it if a slow Webmin / unreachable node-exporter saturates NPM's upstream pool — symptom is 504s on UNRELATED static-asset requests (icons, fonts) because their requests queue behind the slow per-host probes. Raise it on a beefy NPM + manager with many hosts so the Hosts tab paints fully in fewer wall-clock seconds. Default 6. Takes effect on the next /api/me round-trip.

tuning_hosts_idle_fill_interval_seconds.label: Hosts idle-time progressive fill (seconds)
tuning_hosts_idle_fill_interval_seconds.help: Cadence at which the Hosts view trickle-fills off-screen rows in the background while you're idle at the top of the page. Without this, scroll-driven lazy load only fetches what's currently in the viewport — if you stay at the top, the rows below stay un-loaded, and when you finally scroll, every off-screen row spinners up at once. With this set, one not-yet-loaded host is enqueued every N seconds into the SAME shared refresh queue the IntersectionObserver feeds, so by the time you scroll the data is already there. Skips when the tab is hidden, when a host drawer is open, when active scrolling is happening (the IO observer's burst-coalescer takes over), and once every host has loaded. Backend pressure stays bounded by the existing `tuning_hosts_parallel_fetch` worker cap — the trickle never exceeds your fan-out limit. Set 0 to disable entirely (scroll-only lazy load — pre-feature behaviour); 1-2 for fast pre-warm on small fleets; default 3 for an invisible-on-the-backend trickle. Range 0..30. Takes effect on the next /api/me round-trip.

tuning_ai_sidebar_width_px.label: AI sidebar width (px)
tuning_ai_sidebar_width_px.help: Width of the AI Assistant slide-in drawer. The 480 px default fits a 1366 px laptop comfortably; raise it (up to 720 px) on a 4K monitor where the drawer otherwise feels narrow, or lower it (down to 320 px) on a smaller laptop where the drawer covers too much working area. Mobile viewports (< 480 px wide) ignore this value and always render full-width. Range 320..720. Default 480. Takes effect on the next /api/me round-trip — the SPA reads it via `me.client_config.ai_sidebar_width_px` and stamps it onto the drawer root via inline `--ai-sidebar-width` style.

tuning_ai_conversation_export_enabled.label: Allow AI conversation export
tuning_ai_conversation_export_enabled.help: Show the "Export TXT" and "Export JSON" buttons in the AI Assistant sidebar header. When enabled, you can download the current chat as a plain-text transcript or a structured JSON payload — useful for sharing reproducible interactions back with developers / vendors / yourself across machines. Disable to hide the affordance entirely (the conversation still persists in `users.ui_prefs.ai_conversation`; the toggle only governs the SPA-side export UI). Range 0..1. Default 1 (enabled).

tuning_port_scan_default_timeout_seconds.label: Per-port TCP-connect timeout (seconds)
tuning_port_scan_default_timeout_seconds.help: How long the scanner waits for one port's TCP handshake before giving up. Lower (1s) on a quiet LAN to finish faster; raise (5-10s) on flaky / WAN links so genuine listeners aren't marked closed. Per-host overrides on Admin → Hosts win. Range 1..30. Default 2.

tuning_port_scan_default_concurrency.label: Probe concurrency
tuning_port_scan_default_concurrency.help: Parallel TCP-connect probes per scan. Higher = faster scan but louder log footprint on the target. Lower for friendly scans of busy firewalls; higher for quick LAN sweeps. Per-host overrides on Admin → Hosts win. Range 1..256. Default 32.

tuning_port_scan_max_seconds.label: Outer scan budget (seconds)
tuning_port_scan_max_seconds.help: Hard wall-clock cap on a single port-scan request. The scanner is wrapped in `asyncio.wait_for` so a hung target can't pin the worker forever. Default 120s covers a worst-case 1024-port scan with timeout=2 + concurrency=32. Raise for the larger ranges the 11000-port range cap allows (10-15 min on a slow link); lower if your reverse proxy has a tight `proxy_read_timeout`. Range 30..1800.

tuning_port_scan_banner_read_seconds.label: Banner-grab read timeout (seconds)
tuning_port_scan_banner_read_seconds.help: When `banner_grab=true` is set on a scan, the scanner reads up to 256 bytes per open port. Services that don't speak first (HTTP, which expects a request before responding) hit this timeout and get skipped with no banner. Lower for faster scans on services known to greet promptly (SSH / SMTP / IMAP); raise on slow links. Range 1..30. Default 2.

tuning_port_scan_udp_default_timeout_sec.label: Per-port UDP probe timeout (seconds)
tuning_port_scan_udp_default_timeout_sec.help: How long the UDP scanner waits for a reply per port. UDP has no handshake, so a slow service legitimately needs the full window — the default (3s) is longer than TCP's. Lower (1-2s) on a quiet LAN where well-known services reply in milliseconds; raise (5-10s) for satellite links or services known to be slow to respond. Per-host overrides on Admin → Hosts win. Range 1..30. Default 3.

tuning_port_scan_udp_default_concurrency.label: UDP probe concurrency
tuning_port_scan_udp_default_concurrency.help: Parallel UDP probes per scan. Friendlier than TCP's default (32) because UDP traffic is more conspicuous on the network and some services (Asterisk SIP, IPMI BMC) log every datagram they receive. Lower for quiet scans; raise for fast LAN sweeps. Per-host overrides on Admin → Hosts win. Range 1..64. Default 8.

tuning_port_scan_schedule_max_hosts_per_.label: Scheduled refresh — max hosts per tick
tuning_port_scan_schedule_max_hosts_per_.help: Hard ceiling on how many hosts the `port_scan_refresh` schedule kind touches per fire. The runner picks oldest-scanned-first up to this count; the remainder defer to the next fire. Default 5 — keeps a single tick bounded so a fleet of 100 hosts doesn't fan out 100 simultaneous scans the moment a *every-15-minutes* schedule fires for the first time. Lower for very small ticks on slow links; raise on a fast LAN with a small-fleet schedule. Range 1..50. Pairs with the schedule's interval — together they determine the cycle time to cover the whole fleet (interval × ceil(fleet_size / max_hosts_per_tick)).

tuning_port_scan_schedule_min_age_second.label: Scheduled refresh — minimum age before re-scan (seconds)
tuning_port_scan_schedule_min_age_second.help: A host's previous scan must be at least this old before the scheduled runner picks it again. Defence-in-depth against an aggressive every-2-min schedule re-scanning hosts that completed seconds ago — the runner skips any host whose `MAX(host_port_scans.ts)` is younger than this. Default 1800 (30 minutes). Lower for fast-changing fleets where you want the minimum gap close to zero; raise to widen the gap regardless of how often the schedule itself fires. Range 60..86400.

tuning_port_scan_schedule_per_host_concu.label: Scheduled refresh — per-host concurrency
tuning_port_scan_schedule_per_host_concu.help: How many hosts the scheduled runner scans IN PARALLEL within one fire. Default 1 — strictly sequential to keep load low (one host at a time). Raise to 2-4 on quiet networks where the operator wants the tick to complete faster; the per-host scan itself still uses `Probe concurrency` above for its internal port-probe parallelism. Range 1..4.

tuning_sse_heartbeat_seconds.label: SSE heartbeat cadence (seconds)
tuning_sse_heartbeat_seconds.help: How often the /api/events stream emits a `: keepalive` comment so an idle reverse proxy (NPM / Cloudflare / Traefik) doesn't drop the connection on its own idle-keepalive timer. Lower this if your proxy has a tight idle timeout (some defaults are 30s); raise it to cut comment-traffic on long-lived tabs. Default 25 seconds. Takes effect on the NEXT SSE reconnect — already-open connections keep their old cadence until they cycle (every 6h via the lifetime cap, OR after a network drop).

tuning_sse_max_lifetime_seconds.label: SSE connection lifetime cap (seconds)
tuning_sse_max_lifetime_seconds.help: Forces a periodic close + reconnect on each /api/events stream so the cookie-authed tab re-enters the auth middleware, letting the session-cookie's sliding-window refresh land before the 8-hour session hard cap expires. Default 21600 (6 hours), leaving a 1-hour margin for clock skew + heartbeat round-trip on flaky networks. Do NOT raise past 25200 (7 hours) — the upper bound exists to keep the safety margin against the session hard cap. Lower this aggressively (e.g. 3600 = 1 hour) on networks with frequent NAT timeouts; the SPA's EventSource auto-reconnects on the synthetic close, so the operator never sees the cycle.

tuning_webmin_probe_budget_seconds.label: Webmin probe budget (seconds)
tuning_webmin_probe_budget_seconds.help: Outer wall-clock budget for the per-host Webmin probe (system-status + package-updates + mount + net via Miniserv's three-tier XML/JSON/HTML fallback). Caps how long ONE host's Webmin call can block. Used by both the legacy /api/hosts (fleet-wide) AND the per-host /api/hosts/one paths so they stay in lockstep. Lower it (e.g. 10s) on a fleet of fast Miniservs to surface failures faster; raise it for fleets with slow Webmin instances that need the full XML→JSON→HTML fallback chain. Default 20 seconds. Pairs with the 5s negative-cache TTL so a hung Webmin doesn't burn 20s × N parallel calls.

tuning_node_exporter_probe_timeout_secon.label: node-exporter probe timeout (seconds)
tuning_node_exporter_probe_timeout_secon.help: HTTP timeout on every node-exporter scrape, applied uniformly across the legacy /api/hosts fleet probe, the per-host /api/hosts/one probe, the /api/hosts/debug endpoint, AND the lifespan host_metrics_sampler. Lower it on a network where node-exporter usually responds in milliseconds and you want fast-fail on broken hosts; raise it for nodes with deliberately slow exporters or saturated metrics endpoints. Default 10 seconds. The sampler and the request-path used to drift on this knob (sampler 15s vs request-path 10s); both now read this single tunable.

tuning_sse_idle_threshold_seconds.label: SSE freshness threshold (seconds)
tuning_sse_idle_threshold_seconds.help: How long the SPA waits without seeing ANY event (organic or heartbeat) before flipping the live-mode badge to 'polling fallback'. Defence-in-depth on top of EventSource's onerror, which doesn't always fire on silent half-open sockets. Lower it for faster fallback detection on flaky networks (cost: false positives during normal heartbeat gaps); raise it to suppress brief stalls. Default 30 seconds — matches the heartbeat cadence so a stalled stream missing both heartbeats AND organic events triggers fallback within ~2 heartbeat windows. Stored as seconds; SPA multiplies × 1000 for its setInterval comparison.

tuning_pollops_sse_keepalive_seconds.label: Ops poll keepalive when SSE is up (seconds)
tuning_pollops_sse_keepalive_seconds.help: When SSE is healthy, op deltas arrive via push (the `op:created/updated/completed` events), so /api/ops doesn't need fast polling. The SPA's pollOps loop slows from `tuning_ops_poll_interval_seconds` (the fast cadence) to THIS value as a safety-net keepalive — catches a silently-stalled stream that the freshness watchdog hasn't yet detected. Default 30 seconds, lined up with the freshness threshold so the keepalive fires at-or-before the watchdog flips _sseConnected to false. Lower this if you want belt-and-braces faster recovery; raise it to cut idle traffic when SSE is reliable. Stored as seconds; SPA multiplies × 1000 for setTimeout.

tuning_load_busy_max_seconds.label: Spinner / reload watchdog cap (seconds)
tuning_load_busy_max_seconds.help: Hard cap on how long any individual SPA-side busy indicator can stay active — admin reload buttons (gated by `_loadBusy.<key>` in `_runWithBusy`), the topbar Refresh spinner (gated by `loading` / `hostsLoading` in `refresh()` / `loadHosts()`), and the SSE-pill refreshing dot (gated by `cacheRefreshing` / `hubProbing` / `statsRefreshing` mirrored from backend response fields). A hung fetch, dead probe, or stalled background gather can't leave a spinner spinning or a button disabled past this many seconds. Default 30 — matches the published `/api/hosts/one/{id}` probe budget so a healthy fetch always completes well within the cap. Lower (5-10s) if you want aggressive recovery on flaky networks; raise (60-120s) if you have genuinely slow endpoints and would rather see the spinner persist than flicker false-clear. Range 5..600 seconds. Takes effect on the next /api/me round-trip.

tuning_rate_limit_max_failures.label: Login rate-limit failures threshold
tuning_rate_limit_max_failures.help: Maximum consecutive failed login attempts (per IP, AND independently per IP+username pair) before the account locks out. Default 5 — matches OWASP's recommendation for interactive logins. Lower for high-security deployments (e.g. 3); raise for shared-IP environments where multiple operators may collide on bad-typed-passwords (e.g. 10). Resolved per failed-login attempt so a Save in Admin → Config takes effect immediately.

tuning_rate_limit_window_seconds.label: Login rate-limit window (seconds)
tuning_rate_limit_window_seconds.help: Sliding window over which failed login attempts are counted toward the threshold. After N seconds with no further failures the counter resets. Default 900 (15 minutes) — a long enough window to thwart slow-burn brute-force attempts but short enough that a one-off typo from yesterday doesn't carry over. Range 60s (very tight; aggressive recovery) to 86400s (24h; very strict). Resolved per failed-login attempt.

tuning_rate_limit_lockout_seconds.label: Login lockout duration (seconds)
tuning_rate_limit_lockout_seconds.help: Once the failure threshold is hit, how long the IP / IP+username bucket stays locked. Pre-lockout-expiry, every login attempt is rejected without checking the password. Default 900 (15 minutes). Lower for less-paranoid environments where operator typos shouldn't cost much wall-clock; raise (e.g. 3600 = 1 hour, or 86400 = 24h) for high-security deployments where a brute-force attempt should cost the attacker meaningfully. Range 60s-86400s. Resolved per blocked-login check.

tuning_host_provider_cache_ttl_seconds.label: Host-provider cache TTL (seconds)
tuning_host_provider_cache_ttl_seconds.help: How long the batch-provider state (Beszel hub map + Pulse hub map + Webmin creds + active-sources tuple) is cached before the next call re-probes. Settings saves explicitly invalidate this cache so the matter only when an UPSTREAM change happens silently (e.g. a new host appears in Beszel without a settings save). Default 10 seconds. Lower for live-feeling drawer reopens after upstream changes; raise to cut hub probe load on small fleets where the data is mostly static.

tuning_webmin_host_cache_ttl_seconds.label: Webmin per-host success cache TTL (seconds)
tuning_webmin_host_cache_ttl_seconds.help: How long a SUCCESSFUL Webmin probe result is cached per host before the next call re-probes. Burst refreshes (SPA fan-out, drawer reopens) within the window skip the probe entirely and reuse the last known-good stats. Default 30 seconds. Lower for live-feeling drawer reopens; raise to cut Miniserv load on a fleet where most data is stable. Pairs with the failure-cache TTL — a fresh failure on a previously-cached success drops the success entry immediately, so this knob doesn't affect failure-detection latency.

tuning_webmin_host_fail_cache_ttl_second.label: Webmin per-host failure cache TTL (seconds)
tuning_webmin_host_fail_cache_ttl_second.help: How long a FAILED Webmin probe result is cached per host so a SPA fan-out burst doesn't burn 20 seconds × N parallel calls on an unreachable host. Default 5 seconds — tight enough that recovery is felt within one Hosts-tab refresh cycle, long enough to dedupe a fan-out burst. Operators with constantly-flapping Webmin instances may want 30s+ to suppress the spam; operators wanting fastest possible recovery detection can drop to 1s.

tuning_host_metrics_probe_concurrency.label: Host metrics sampler concurrency
tuning_host_metrics_probe_concurrency.help: Maximum parallel node-exporter scrapes the lifespan host_metrics_sampler runs per tick. Default 8 — comfortable on a 60-host fleet through 8 workers in ~3 batches without saturating the manager. Lower on a Pi-class manager where 8 parallel HTTP scrapes saturate the network or CPU; raise on a beefy host or a fleet of many hosts where serialised batches push past the 5-minute interval.

tuning_auth_failure_cooldown_seconds.label: Auth-failure cool-down (seconds)
tuning_auth_failure_cooldown_seconds.help: Per-(host, user) cool-down applied after a Webmin or SSH probe gets a 401. During the cool-down, OmniGrid skips that host's probe entirely (returning the cached failure) instead of re-burning a doomed call. Shared between Webmin and SSH so a single Save propagates to both. Default 300 seconds (5 minutes) — long enough to avoid lockout cascades on bad credentials, short enough that operators don't have to wait an hour after fixing a typo.

tuning_ping_interval_seconds.label: Ping sampler interval (seconds)
tuning_ping_interval_seconds.help: Cadence at which the lifespan `ping_sampler` runs TCP-connect (or ICMP, when enabled) probes against every curated ping-enabled host and writes one row per host into `ping_samples`. 0 = use the global `tuning_stats_sample_interval_seconds` (default 300s) — keeps Ping on the same heartbeat as the data-bearing samplers. Distinct knob exists because operators often want Ping to tick FASTER than the other providers (sub-minute reachability checks against routers / 5G modems) without bumping the global cadence. Range 0..3600. Default 0 (= inherit).

tuning_ping_concurrency.label: Ping probe concurrency
tuning_ping_concurrency.help: Maximum parallel ping probes the sampler runs in one tick. Default 16 — fits a 60-host fleet through 16 workers in ~4 sequential batches. Lower if probes are saturating the network's outbound concurrency budget (NPM, ISP modem, etc.); raise on a beefy host with many opted-in hosts to keep the per-tick wall-clock under the interval. Range 1..128.

tuning_ping_probe_timeout_seconds.label: Ping per-probe timeout (seconds)
tuning_ping_probe_timeout_seconds.help: Wall-clock budget for ONE TCP-connect attempt (or one ICMP echo round-trip). Each tick fires 3 probes per host; this knob caps each one independently. Default 2 seconds — enough for cross-WAN latencies but tight enough to not stall the sampler on dead hosts. Lower if your fleet is all on-LAN (sub-millisecond RTTs); raise if you're probing hosts over a satellite link or a flaky upstream. Range 1..30.

tuning_ping_cooldown_seconds.label: Ping cool-down on consecutive timeouts (seconds)
tuning_ping_cooldown_seconds.help: After TWO consecutive timeout-batches against the same host:port, the probe arms a cool-down — subsequent ticks skip that pair until the cool-down expires. Cuts wire traffic against permanently-unreachable hosts without removing them from the chart entirely. Default 300 seconds (5 minutes). Lower for fast-recovery detection; raise to suppress probes against a host that's been down for hours. Range 30..3600.

tuning_snmp_probe_timeout_seconds.label: SNMP per-OID timeout (seconds)
tuning_snmp_probe_timeout_seconds.help: pysnmp's per-OID UDP timeout — applied to EACH GET / GETBULK round-trip independently. Default 5 seconds — enough for slow embedded devices to ack a single packet without stalling the gather pipeline on a truly dead host. This is NOT the wall-clock budget for the whole probe; that's `SNMP wall-clock probe budget` below. Lower this for fast-fail behaviour on dead hosts; raise it on flaky high-RTT links. Range 1..60.

tuning_snmp_wall_clock_budget_seconds.label: SNMP wall-clock probe budget (seconds)
tuning_snmp_wall_clock_budget_seconds.help: Total wall-clock budget for ONE SNMP probe against ONE host. The probe fans out ~60 OID operations (sysName / hrStorage / hrProcessorLoad / ifTable / ENTITY-MIB plus vendor-private MIBs for Dell / Cisco / APC / UCD / Synology / HP printer / Dell-RAC); pysnmp serialises the wire-level UDP packets through one transport so total wall-clock is roughly (round-trips × RTT). Operator-reported case: WD MyCloud / network printer / low-power NAS with ~500 ms RTT × 60 ops ≈ 30 s, blowing past the previous hardcoded 14 s budget on every cycle and tripping the auto-pause threshold. Default 60 s leaves margin for the slowest legitimate device. Lower if your SNMP fleet is uniformly fast (LAN switches answer in < 5 s easily); raise to 120-300 s if you have unusually slow embedded gear. Range 5..600.

tuning_snmp_per_host_walk_concurrency.label: SNMP per-host walk concurrency
tuning_snmp_per_host_walk_concurrency.help: Caps how many _snmp_get / _snmp_walk operations OmniGrid sends in parallel against ONE host. Default 1 (fully serialised — CLI-equivalent wire-level pattern) protects slow BMC-class agents (iDRAC9, IPMI, low-power embedded snmpd) from UDP receive-queue overflow when 60+ concurrent bulk requests arrive simultaneously — observed pattern is `[snmp] WALK errorIndication: No SNMP response received before timeout` for ~15 of ~60 OID branches per probe. Raise to 8-16 if your fleet is uniformly fast (Cisco / Synology / linux net-snmp): a 60-OID probe at 100 ms RTT serialises to ~6 s wall-clock at concurrency=1 vs ~0.5 s at concurrency=16. Range 1..16. Default 1.

tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_dell.label: SNMP walk concurrency — Dell (auto-detect override)
tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_dell.help: Per-vendor walk_concurrency default that kicks in when sysDescr auto-detect resolves to Dell ONLY (no per-host override set, no other vendor matched). Dell iDRAC9 / iDRAC10 / Cisco IMC / Supermicro IPMI handle parallel queries fine and benefit dramatically from > 1 because pysnmp's per-walk overhead compounds 67× at concurrency=1. Recommended: 4 for a homogeneous Dell fleet. Default 0 (disabled — falls through to the generic per-host walk_concurrency above). Range 0..16.

tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_cisco.label: SNMP walk concurrency — Cisco (auto-detect override)
tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_cisco.help: Per-vendor walk_concurrency default for Cisco IOS / NX-OS / IOS-XR / IOS-XE auto-detected agents. Cisco gear typically handles parallel queries well — recommended 8 for a Cisco fleet. Default 0 (disabled). Range 0..16.

tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_synology.label: SNMP walk concurrency — Synology (auto-detect override)
tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_synology.help: Per-vendor walk_concurrency default for Synology DiskStation / RackStation auto-detected agents. Synology DSM's snmpd handles parallel queries reasonably well — recommended 4. Default 0 (disabled). Range 0..16.

tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_ucd.label: SNMP walk concurrency — UCD/net-snmp (auto-detect override)
tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_ucd.help: Per-vendor walk_concurrency default for net-snmp / ucd-snmp / OpenWrt auto-detected agents. Linux net-snmp is generally fast — recommended 8. Default 0 (disabled). Range 0..16.

tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_printer.label: SNMP walk concurrency — Printers (auto-detect override)
tuning_snmp_walk_concurrency_printer.help: Per-vendor walk_concurrency default for HP / Brother / Epson / Canon / Kyocera / Ricoh / Xerox / Lexmark / Konica Minolta / Samsung-printer-line auto-detected agents. Printers usually drop UDP packets under parallel load — leave at 0 (= use the safety-floor generic default of 1) unless your specific model is known to handle parallel queries. Default 0 (disabled). Range 0..16.

tuning_snmp_concurrency.label: SNMP probe concurrency
tuning_snmp_concurrency.help: Maximum parallel SNMP probes the gather + per-host-merge paths run in one tick. Default 16 — comfortable on a 50-host SNMP fleet through 16 workers in ~3 batches. Lower if your network gear's CPU is fragile (some older switches lock up under > 8 parallel SNMP requests); raise on a beefy fleet of well-provisioned UPSes / routers. Range 1..128.

tuning_snmp_host_cache_ttl_seconds.label: SNMP per-host success cache TTL (seconds)
tuning_snmp_host_cache_ttl_seconds.help: How long a SUCCESSFUL SNMP probe result is cached per host before the next call re-probes. Burst refreshes (SPA fan-out, drawer reopens) within the window skip the probe entirely and reuse the last known-good stats. Default 30 seconds. Lower for live-feeling drawer reopens; raise to cut SNMP probe load on a fleet of slow-responding UPSes / network gear. Pairs with the failure-cache TTL — a fresh failure on a previously-cached success drops the success entry immediately.

tuning_snmp_host_fail_cache_ttl_seconds.label: SNMP per-host failure cache TTL (seconds)
tuning_snmp_host_fail_cache_ttl_seconds.help: How long a FAILED SNMP probe result is cached per host so a SPA fan-out burst doesn't re-burn the timeout × N parallel calls on an unreachable host. Default 5 seconds — tight enough that recovery is felt within one Hosts-tab refresh cycle, long enough to dedupe a fan-out burst. Operators with constantly-flapping SNMP devices may want 30s+ to suppress the spam; operators wanting fastest possible recovery detection can drop to 1s.

tuning_snmp_unreachable_cooldown_seconds.label: SNMP unreachable cool-down (seconds)
tuning_snmp_unreachable_cooldown_seconds.help: After a single SNMP probe times out, the per-(host, port) probe arms a cool-down — subsequent ticks skip that pair until the cool-down expires. UDP timeouts are slower to detect than auth-failure 401s, so SNMP arms after one strike (Webmin / SSH wait for two consecutive 401s before arming). Cuts wire traffic against permanently-unreachable devices without removing them from the chart entirely. Default 300 seconds (5 minutes). Lower for fast-recovery detection on flaky links; raise to suppress probes against a host that's been down for hours. SNMP used to share the auth-failure cool-down knob with Webmin / SSH; this dedicated tunable lets you tune SNMP independently. Range 30..3600.

tuning_snmp_sample_interval_seconds.label: SNMP sample interval (seconds)
tuning_snmp_sample_interval_seconds.help: How often the host_metrics_sampler probes SNMP-enabled hosts. SEPARATE from the global Stats sample interval so SNMP gear can run on a different cadence than Beszel / node-exporter agents — printers can poll hourly, switches every minute. Default 0 means 'use the global Stats sample interval' (legacy behaviour); any value 30..3600 overrides the global for SNMP probes only. Lowering this speeds up chart freshness on managed network gear at the cost of more SNMP traffic; raising it cuts traffic against rarely-changing devices. Sampler reads per-tick so changes take effect without restart.

tuning_snmp_failure_pause_rounds.label: SNMP auto-pause threshold (rounds)
tuning_snmp_failure_pause_rounds.help: After this many consecutive failed SNMP sampler rounds against a host, the (snmp, host) pair is marked auto-paused — subsequent probes are SKIPPED entirely until the operator clicks Resume on the SNMP chip in the host drawer. The in-memory cool-down still throttles individual failures (default 5 min between attempts); this knob is the higher-level 'this device is broken, stop probing it' signal. Default 5 = ~25 min of failures at the default 5-min cadence before the chip goes amber. Lower (e.g. 3) for fast-fail on critical gear; raise (e.g. 20) to ride out long maintenance windows. Set 0 to disable auto-pause entirely (cool-down still applies). Range 0..50.

tuning_webmin_failure_pause_rounds.label: Webmin auto-pause threshold (rounds)
tuning_webmin_failure_pause_rounds.help: After this many consecutive failed Webmin probes against a host, the (webmin, host) pair is marked auto-paused — subsequent probes are SKIPPED entirely until the operator clicks Resume on the Webmin chip in the host drawer. The 5-min auth-failure cool-down still throttles credential lockout independently. Cool-down responses (HTTP 401 backoff) do NOT count toward the threshold — only real probe failures (timeout, network error, agent rejection). Default 5. Set 0 to disable auto-pause entirely. Range 0..50.

tuning_beszel_failure_pause_rounds.label: Beszel auto-pause threshold (rounds)
tuning_beszel_failure_pause_rounds.help: After this many consecutive rounds where the Beszel hub fetch SUCCEEDED but this specific host was missing from the hub map (or reported status=down/paused), the (beszel, host) pair is marked auto-paused — subsequent merges are SKIPPED until the operator clicks Resume on the Beszel chip in the host drawer. Hub-level outages (entire hub fetch fails) do NOT count toward the threshold so a single hub blip doesn't cascade-pause every host. Default 5. Set 0 to disable. Range 0..50.

tuning_beszel_probe_timeout_seconds.label: Beszel probe timeout (seconds)
tuning_beszel_probe_timeout_seconds.help: Wall-clock timeout on each Beszel hub fetch (`probe_hub` — covers `systems` + `system_stats` + `systemd_services` collections). Caps how long ONE tick can wait on a stuck PocketBase before giving up. Lower (5–10s) for fast-fail on a flaky hub; raise (30–60s) for slow remote hubs reachable over a WAN link. Range 1..120. Default 15.

tuning_beszel_sample_interval_seconds.label: Beszel sampler interval (seconds)
tuning_beszel_sample_interval_seconds.help: Cadence at which the lifespan `host_beszel_sampler` writes one row per Beszel-tracked host into the local `host_beszel_samples` table. 0 = use the global `tuning_stats_sample_interval_seconds` (default 300s) — same fallback Pulse / Webmin samplers use, keeps the four samplers ticking on the same heartbeat. Distinct knob exists so a fleet with a noisy / large Beszel hub can throttle Beszel sampling independently. Range 0..3600. Default 0 (= inherit).

tuning_pulse_failure_pause_rounds.label: Pulse auto-pause threshold (rounds)
tuning_pulse_failure_pause_rounds.help: After this many consecutive rounds where the Pulse hub fetch SUCCEEDED but this specific host was missing from the hub map (or reported status=down/paused/unreachable), the (pulse, host) pair is marked auto-paused — subsequent merges are SKIPPED until the operator clicks Resume on the Pulse chip. Hub-level outages don't count. Default 5. Set 0 to disable. Range 0..50.

tuning_pulse_probe_timeout_seconds.label: Pulse hub probe timeout (seconds)
tuning_pulse_probe_timeout_seconds.help: Per-fetch timeout for the Pulse `/api/state` hub call. One hub fetch covers every Pulse-mapped host, so this knob bounds the SAMPLER tick wall-clock and the synchronous probe path used by /api/hosts/list. Default 15s — Pulse responds sub-second on a healthy hub but slow proxies / under-provisioned PVE clusters can take longer. Lower (e.g. 5s) on a fast LAN; raise (e.g. 30s) if the hub is behind a slow Cloudflare Tunnel. Range 1..120s. Takes effect on the next sampler tick.

tuning_webmin_probe_timeout_seconds.label: Webmin per-host probe timeout (seconds)
tuning_webmin_probe_timeout_seconds.help: Per-fetch timeout for the Webmin Miniserv probe in the host_webmin_sampler. Default 8s matches the previous hardcoded fallback. Lower (e.g. 5s) on a fast LAN; raise (e.g. 30s) if Miniserv runs behind a slow proxy or returns chunked HTML responses that take time to parse. Range 1..120s. Takes effect on the next sampler tick.

tuning_node_exporter_failure_pause_round.label: node-exporter auto-pause threshold (rounds)
tuning_node_exporter_failure_pause_round.help: After this many consecutive failed per-host node-exporter scrapes, the (node_exporter, host) pair is marked auto-paused — subsequent scrapes are SKIPPED until the operator clicks Resume on the chip. A failure here means HTTP error, timeout, or `exporter_error` response. Default 5. Set 0 to disable. Range 0..50.

tuning_ping_failure_pause_rounds.label: Ping auto-pause threshold (rounds)
tuning_ping_failure_pause_rounds.help: After this many consecutive SAMPLER-LEVEL errors (DNS NXDOMAIN, ICMP permission denied from runner, transport setup failure — NOT plain alive=False which is the actual data the operator wants surfaced), the (ping, host) pair is marked auto-paused. Default 0 (disabled) so existing deploys don't see ping chips spuriously paused on a normally-down host. Operators concerned about runtime sampler errors can opt in by raising the value. Range 0..50.

tuning_stat_bar_warn_pct.label: Stat-bar warning threshold (percent)
tuning_stat_bar_warn_pct.help: Percent at which CPU / Memory / Disk bars flip from green to amber across the SPA. Default 60 — leaves headroom on bursty workloads while making sustained load visible. Lower (e.g. 50) for over-provisioned hosts where any sustained load is interesting; raise (e.g. 75) on hosts where high steady utilisation is normal (build farms, batch processing). Must stay ≤ the crit threshold so the green→amber→red progression remains monotonic. Range 30..90. Resolved per render; an Admin → Config save reflects on the next /api/me round-trip.

tuning_stat_bar_crit_pct.label: Stat-bar critical threshold (percent)
tuning_stat_bar_crit_pct.help: Percent at which CPU / Memory / Disk bars flip from amber to red. Default 85 — the typical "investigate before saturation" signal. Lower (e.g. 75) for fleets where 80% sustained means a rebalance; raise (e.g. 95) for over-provisioned hosts where amber stays green-equivalent. Must be ≥ the warning threshold. Range 50..99. Resolved per render.

tuning_ssh_default_port.label: SSH default port
tuning_ssh_default_port.help: Default TCP port for the SSH terminal + run-command flows. Consumed when a curated host's `ssh.port` is unset. Default 22 (standard SSH). Range 1..65535. Live — takes effect on the next SSH invocation; no restart needed.

tuning_snmp_default_port.label: SNMP default port
tuning_snmp_default_port.help: Default UDP port for SNMP queries. Consumed when a curated host's `snmp.port` is unset. Default 161 (standard SNMP). Range 1..65535. Live — takes effect on the next probe cycle; no restart needed.

tuning_ping_default_port.label: Ping default port
tuning_ping_default_port.help: Default TCP port for the Ping host-stats provider's TCP-connect probe. Consumed when a curated host's `ping.port` is unset. Default 443 (HTTPS — universally reachable through firewalls). Range 1..65535. Live — takes effect on the next sampler tick.

tuning_ping_packet_interval_ms.label: ICMP inter-packet spacing (milliseconds)
tuning_ping_packet_interval_ms.help: Time between successive ICMP echo packets in a single ping burst. Sub-quarter-second spacing can trip IDS / anti-flood rules on commercial firewalls (UBNT EdgeRouter, Sophos, Palo Alto). Raise if your monitored targets sit behind aggressive flood-protection; lower for tighter sampling on a private LAN. Default 200ms — matches icmplib's own default. Range 100..2000ms. Takes effect on the next ping_sampler tick.

tuning_ssh_terminal_connect_timeout_seco.label: SSH terminal connect timeout (seconds)
tuning_ssh_terminal_connect_timeout_seco.help: Wall-clock cap on the initial TCP+SSH handshake for the interactive SSH terminal (Admin → SSH → terminal). Once connected, the login-timeout tunable below covers the auth handshake. Raise for WAN-routed targets on slow links; lower for strict-watchdog setups where a stuck connect should fail fast. Default 20s. Range 5..120s. Takes effect on the next terminal-open click.

tuning_ssh_terminal_login_timeout_second.label: SSH terminal login timeout (seconds)
tuning_ssh_terminal_login_timeout_second.help: Wall-clock cap on the post-connect SSH authentication phase (PubKey attempts, password prompt, banner exchange). Distinct from the connect timeout above. Same trade-off — raise for slow-auth servers (LDAP-backed sshd, PAM password lookups), lower for tighter failure detection. Default 20s. Range 5..120s. Takes effect on the next terminal-open click.

tuning_ssh_close_timeout_seconds.label: SSH connection-close wait timeout (seconds)
tuning_ssh_close_timeout_seconds.help: Wall-clock cap on `conn.wait_closed()` after a terminal session ends. Default 5s. Lower on flaky networks where the FIN never arrives (close blocks forever, audit row stays open); raise if you genuinely care about clean teardown and tolerate longer audit-close delays. Range 1..60. Takes effect on the next session-close.

tuning_oidc_http_timeout_seconds.label: OIDC HTTP timeout (seconds)
tuning_oidc_http_timeout_seconds.help: Wall-clock cap for every OIDC outbound HTTP call — discovery-doc GET, JWKS fetch, token exchange at callback, and the admin Test-connection probe. Default 15s. Lower (8-10s) on fast-fail watchdog deploys; raise (30-60s) when the IdP is behind a slow corporate proxy or VPN. Range 2..120. Takes effect on the next round-trip.

tuning_gather_client_timeout_seconds.label: Gather fan-out HTTP timeout (seconds)
tuning_gather_client_timeout_seconds.help: Outer AsyncClient wall-clock for the main Portainer fan-out (containers + services + stacks + tasks + nodes walks). Default 60s. Lower on fast-fail deploys with a healthy Portainer; raise for large fleets (200+ containers) where the registry-digest probe inside each walk adds wall-clock. Range 5..600. Takes effect on the next gather.

tuning_gather_orphan_probe_timeout_secon.label: Gather orphan-probe timeout (seconds)
tuning_gather_orphan_probe_timeout_secon.help: Per-call wall-clock for the cross-host container-inspect probe that resolves which Swarm node owns an orphaned container (the inner loop tries each hostname in turn; a 404 should come back fast, a 200 wins immediately). Default 3s. Lower (1-2s) for tight fan-outs on responsive networks; raise (5-10s) for sluggish workers. Range 1..30. Takes effect on the next orphan-probe pass.

tuning_pulse_sample_interval_seconds.label: Pulse sampler interval (seconds)
tuning_pulse_sample_interval_seconds.help: Cadence at which the lifespan `host_pulse_sampler` writes one row per Pulse-tracked guest into the local `host_pulse_samples` table. 0 = use the global `tuning_stats_sample_interval_seconds` (default 300s) — keeps Pulse on the same heartbeat as the other samplers. Distinct knob exists so a fleet with a noisy / large Pulse hub (Proxmox cluster with hundreds of guests) can throttle Pulse sampling independently. Range 0..3600. Default 0 (= inherit).

tuning_node_exporter_sample_interval_sec.label: node-exporter sampler interval (seconds)
tuning_node_exporter_sample_interval_sec.help: Cadence at which `host_metrics_sampler` probes node-exporter on each curated host and writes one row per host into `host_metrics_samples`. 0 = use the global `tuning_stats_sample_interval_seconds` (default 300s). > 0 overrides per-NE so operators can sample node-exporter at a finer cadence (e.g. 60s for tight CPU / mem / disk traces) while keeping the hub-based providers on the slower default. The outer sampler tick is auto-resolved to the MIN of NE / SNMP / global so neither sub-sampler gets starved by a slow override on a sibling. Range 0..3600. Default 0 (= inherit).

tuning_swarm_autoheal_cooldown_minutes.label: Swarm autoheal cooldown (minutes)
tuning_swarm_autoheal_cooldown_minutes.help: Minimum minutes between consecutive auto-restart actions by the `swarm_agent_health` schedule kind on the SAME agent service — prevents a thrashing agent from pinning the manager in a restart loop. Default 30 min; range 1..1440 (24h max). Notify-mode bypasses the cooldown; only the restart action consumes it.

tuning_webmin_sampler_budget_seconds.label: Webmin sampler tick budget (seconds)
tuning_webmin_sampler_budget_seconds.help: Outer wall-clock cap for one Webmin sampler tick (fans out per-host `_probe_one_host` calls). Without an outer cap a single wedged Webmin host can starve the whole tick. 0 = auto-derive from `probe_timeout × hosts capped at 5 min` — safe default for fleets < 60 hosts. Operators with very large fleets can pin a higher value if their per-host probe is fast and they want a bigger fan-out window. Range 0..600s. Resolved per-tick.

tuning_notifications_poll_interval_secon.label: Notifications popup poll cadence (seconds)
tuning_notifications_poll_interval_secon.help: How often the notifications popup polls /api/notifications when the SSE stream is disconnected AND the popup is open. Under normal conditions the popup live-updates via SSE — this knob ONLY controls the fall-back when SSE drops. Default 30s. Operators on slow connections / power-saving devices may want to raise (e.g. 60s); operators wanting near-live recovery after an SSE drop may want 5-10s. Range 5..300s. Takes effect on the next popup open.

tuning_ai_conversation_persist_interval_.label: AI conversation persist cadence (ms)
tuning_ai_conversation_persist_interval_.help: How often the AI sidebar checks for changes in the active conversation and writes the snapshot through to /api/me/ui-prefs. Default 2000ms (2 seconds). Operators on slow connections or low-power devices can raise this (e.g. 5000-10000ms) to reduce sync churn; aggressive setups wanting near-instant recovery on accidental tab close can lower toward the 500ms floor. Range 500..30000ms. Takes effect on the NEXT page load — the interval doesn't re-arm its cadence mid-session.

totp.title: Two-factor authentication (TOTP)
totp.subtitle: Policy controls for local-auth users. Authentik users skip every TOTP path — their IdP handles MFA. Each enrolled user keeps their own QR / backup codes; admins can disable a user's 2FA from the Users tab when a device is lost.
totp.allowed_hint: Master switch. When off, TOTP enrolment is hidden from Profile AND existing TOTP-enrolled users are NOT prompted for a code at login. Their secret stays in the DB for when you flip it back on.
totp.passkeys_allowed_hint: Master switch for WebAuthn / FIDO2 passkeys. When off, the passkey "Add" button hides in Profile AND already-enrolled passkeys are NOT offered at login. Existing rows stay in the DB so the same user can revoke them, and login resumes when you flip it back on.
totp.required_hint: Forced enrolment runs at login: a user without 2FA gets the QR + verification step before the cookie is issued.
totp.max_failures_hint: How many consecutive wrong TOTP codes from one user trigger a temporary lockout on their account. Lower it (3-5) for stricter brute-force defence; raise (8-15) when users are typing codes manually from a phone authenticator and fat-fingers happen regularly. Backup codes count against the same threshold. Lockout duration is set by the next field.
totp.lockout_minutes_hint: How long a user is locked out after hitting the failure threshold above. Short (5-15 min) keeps legitimate fat-finger lockouts brief; long (60-1440 min) makes brute-force attacks impractical. The lockout clears on its own when the timer expires; an admin can also reset a locked user via Admin → Users.

users.totp_disable_title: Clear this user's TOTP enrolment (use when they've lost their authenticator device).

users.totp_unforce_title: Drop the per-user 2FA requirement. Global policy still applies.
